semanticCore: Panting (like a dog), gasping for air, tongue out, fatigue, or thirst
quranicRelevance: Denier panting like a dog (Yalhas - 'yalhath aw tatrukhu yalhath').

keyDerivations.meaning: To pant, gasp for air, protrude the tongue in exhaustion/heat, or breathe heavily

figurativeOrRareUsages: فَمَثَلُهُ كَمَثَلِ الْكَلْبِ إِنْ تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْهِ يَلْهَثْ أَوْ تَتْرُكْهُ يَلْهَثْ (7:176): So his parable is like that of the dog: if you chase him, he pants/gasp, or if you leave him alone, he pants (chronic spiritual restlessness).
discardedNoise: Modern gasping for breath during asthma attacks.
summaryEn: The root represents panting (Lahth), using the restless dog's panting tongue to illustrate the persistent misery of those who throw away divine verses.
summaryAr: يمثل هذا الجذر تنفس الكلب الحار الخارج من فمه مع إخراج اللسان تعباً أو عطشاً (لهث)، مشبهاً الشقي المعرض عن الهداية بالكلب يلهث بكل حال.
rawText: إِلْتَهَثَ 8 see 1. فِى سَكْرَةٍ مَلْهَثَةٌ In intoxication is a cause, or an occasion, of لهث [i. e., thirst; or putting forth the tongue by reason of thirst]. (TA, from a trad.) لَهِثَ 1 , aor. ﹷ, inf. n. لَهَثٌ and لَهَاثٌ and لَهَثَانٌ, He thirsted; was thirsty: (S, K:) or thirst heated his belly, or inside. (L.) === لَهَثَ, aor. ﹷ, inf. n. لَهْثٌ and لُهَاثٌ; (S, K;) and لَهِثَ, aor. ﹷ; (TA;) and 🡻التهث; (K;) He (a dog, S, and a man, and, in the case of the second verb, a bird also, TA) put forth his tongue, by reason of thirst, or fatigue, or weariness: (S, K:) or لَهَثَ signifies his breath (نفس) rose, by reason of fatigue, or weariness: or he (a dog) put forth his tongue, by reason of thirst: and he (a man) was fatigued, or weary. (TA.) لَهْثَانُ Thirsty: (S, K:) fem. لَهْثَى. (S.) لُهَاثٌ The heat, or burning, of thirst; (S, K;) vehemence of thirst. (TA.) === ‡ The severity, or vehemence, of death. (K, TA.) ___ See لُهْثَةٌ. لُهَاثِىٌّ † A man (TA) having many red moles (خِيلَان) on his face: (Fr, K:) from لُهَاثٌ, signifying the “ specks in palm-leaves. ” (Fr.) لُهَّاثٌ [pl. of لَاهِثٌ?] Manufacturers, with palm-leaves, of the kind of baskets called دَوَاخِلُّ, (K,) pl. of دَوْخَلَّهٌ, in which dates are put. (AA.) لُهْثَةٌ Thirst. (K.) === Fatigue. (AA, K.) === The red speck in a palm-leaf, (K,) which you see when you split it. (TA.) === لُهَاثٌ [for لِهَاثٌ, pl. of لُهْثَةٌ,] The specks in palmleaves: (Fr, K:) the red specks in palm-leaves, when they are split: (Fr, TA:) accord. to rule, it should be لِهَاثٌ, like نِقَاطٌ. (K.)
